krama: नमो॑ मी॒ढुष्ट॑माय । मी॒ढुष्ट॑माय च । चेषु॑मते । इषु॑मते च । च इति॑ च ॥

sayana.english: 'Midhustamah'—one who rains (shatters) excessively in the form of a cloud. 'Ishuman'—the holder of arrows.

dhyana.english: May one meditate on Hara, who has three eyes, is seated on a bull, accompanied by the lord of hosts (Ganesha), and whose entire body is smeared with ashes and adorned with a crown of matted hair.

bhatta_bhaskara.english:
'Namo midhustamaya cha'—to the greatest shedder/pourer. [Grammatical note]. 'Ishumate cha'—to the one with excellent arrows, or holding arrows in hand; it means a great hero. (7)

Salutation to the short and to the dwarf.

dhyana.english: May one meditate on Hara, who has three eyes, is seated on a bull, accompanied by the lord of hosts (Ganesha), and whose entire body is smeared with ashes and adorned with a crown of matted hair.

abhinava_shankara.sanskrit:
धन्वा, 'स तथोक्ततथेत्युक्त्वा विष्णुं कृत्वा शरोत्तमम् ।
शाल्यमत्रं तथा कृत्वा धनुर्मेहमकारयत् ॥ कृत्वा च धनुरो-
कारं साविरी ज्यां महेश्वरः' इत्यादिभारतोक्तवेद्मणवमेरु-
पिनाकाद्यनेकधनुर्युक्तायेत्यर्थे। अनेन 'तेषामसुराणां त्रिः
पुर आसन्' इति श्रुतिप्रसिद्धत्रिपुरसंहारमहिह्मा दर्शितः ॥

      नमो गिरिशाय च शिपिविष्टाय च
            नमो मीढुष्टमाय चेषुमते च ॥

      अथ द्वाभ्यां त्रिमूर्त्यात्मकत्वम्, विश्वाधिकत्वं च दर्शयति
— नमो गिरिशायेति । गिरिः कैलाशः, सोऽस्यास्तीति
गिरिशः, लोमादित्वान्मत्वर्थीयः शः, तद्रूपाय इत्यर्थे ।
शिपिविष्टः विष्णुः, 'विष्णुः शिपिविष्टः' इति श्रुवेः;
विष्णुरूपायेत्यर्थे। मीढुष्टमः अतिशयेन वीर्येणेषच्छकः जग-
त्वष्टा हिरण्यगर्भः, तद्रूपायेत्यर्थे। अनेन 'स ब्रह्मा च
हरिः सेन्द्रः सोऽक्षरः परमः खरात्' इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धत्रि-
मूर्त्यात्मकत्वमुक्तं भवति। प्रशस्तः इषुरस्यास्तीति इषुपान्,
प्रशंसायां मतुप् ; प्राशस्त्यं च इषोः 'स इषुक् समस्कु-
वेताभिप्रमनीक^ सोमं^ शल्यं विष्णुं तेजनम्' इत्यादिश्रु-
त्युक्तं द्रष्टव्यम् । तेन च सर्वदेवानामपि त्रिपुरसंहारीयं भ-
abhinava_shankara.english:
dhānvan (the hundred-bowed one). As the Mahābhārata describes: "Having said 'so be it' to Viṣṇu and made Him the supreme arrow, having fashioned the salt-mountain (śālya) as the arrowhead, the Great Lord crafted the bow of the syllable Om; and the Great God made the Gāyatrī as the bowstring" — indicating the Lord who is equipped with many bows such as the cosmic Meru, Pināka, and others. Through this name, the glory of the destruction of the Triple Cities (tripura-saṃhāra) is indicated, well-attested in the Śruti: "There were three cities of those demons."

      Homage to Giriśa and to Śipiviṣṭa;
      homage to Mīḍhuṣṭama and to Iṣumat.

      Now with two [mantras], the commentator shows the Lord's nature as the Trinity (trimūrti) and His supremacy over the universe. "Namo giriśāya" — the mountain (giri) is Kailāsa; he who possesses it [as his abode] is Giriśa, with the suffix śa in the sense of "possessing" in the class of derivatives known as lomādi (a Pāṇinian class); the meaning is: "to Him who is of that form [i.e., the one who dwells on Kailāsa]." Śipiviṣṭa is Viṣṇu — "Viṣṇu is Śipiviṣṭa," says the Śruti — meaning: "to Him in the form of Viṣṇu." Mīḍhuṣṭama is Hiraṇyagarbha, the creator of the universe, who is supreme in virility (vīrya) and is possessed of a slightly lesser degree of that power; the meaning is: "to Him in the form of Hiraṇyagarbha." Through this, the nature of the Lord as the Trinity — "He is Brahmā, He is Hari, He is Indra, He is the Imperishable Supreme" — as declared by the Śruti, is affirmed.

      He who possesses excellent arrows (iṣavaḥ) is Iṣumat (the arrow-possessor), with the suffix matuP in the sense of distinction (praśaṃsā); and the excellence of the arrow is stated in the Śruti: "That arrow he fashioned, aimed it at the demons, Soma as the shaft, Viṣṇu as the point" — and so forth. Through this, the participation of all the gods themselves as accessories in the Lord's campaign of Tripura-destruction is acknowledged, and thereby
